headings.text: Cracked Foundations: How Japan's Retrofit Law Left 340,000 Buildings Behind
I. The Law That Wouldn't Stick
III. The Subsidy That Doesn't Reach
Four views of the retrofit gap

paragraphs.text: A four-decade-old seismic code was supposed to bring every pre-1981 structure up to standard. Three years of records, 18,000 inspection reports, and 62 interviews show why the deadline keeps moving — and who keeps paying the price.

On the third floor of a 1974 reinforced-concrete apartment block in Higashinada Ward, a hairline crack runs the length of the south-facing load-bearing wall. The crack was first photographed by a municipal inspector in 1996, eleven months after the Great Hanshin earthquake reduced large portions of Kobe to rubble. Nearly three decades later, the crack is wider, the building is still occupied by 64 households, and its official retrofit status remains, in the city's filing system, "pending owner consultation."1

It is not an outlier. A three-year investigation by Meridian Fault Dispatch — drawing on prefectural inspection databases, Ministry of Land records obtained through Article 76 requests, and on-site surveys in 14 wards across Tokyo, Osaka, Kobe and Sendai — has identified at least 340,000 buildings nationwide that fall below the 1981 New Seismic Standard but have never been retrofitted, despite forty-three years of national policy commitments and the equivalent of ¥2.1 trillion in cumulative subsidy allocations.2

The 1981 code, known in industry as the shin-taishin, was Japan's response to the 1978 Miyagi quake. It rewrote the lateral-load formulas for every new structure over three storeys, and — crucially — it set a long-horizon target: every pre-1981 building in the country was, eventually, to be brought up to standard. Successive governments translated "eventually" into 2015, then 2020, then 2025. The current Ministry projection, quietly revised in March, now reads 2035.3

The retrofit law's central instrument is voluntary. Local governments may compel diagnostic inspections only for designated "seismic emergency routes" and for buildings classified as essential — hospitals, schools, government offices. For the country's 5.9 million pre-1981 residential and commercial structures, compliance is a matter of owner initiative, partially defrayed by municipal subsidies that typically cover 23 to 50 percent of retrofit costs.4
"We knew in 1996 that the law had a hole in it. The Hanshin quake should have closed that hole. Instead, we made the hole bigger by calling it a subsidy."

Iwase's account, given over two days of interviews in Yokohama, is corroborated by internal ministry memos from 1998 and 2002 reviewed by Meridian. The memos describe a retrofit uptake rate of "between 0.4 and 1.1 percent annually" for non-mandatory structures — a pace at which clearing the pre-1981 backlog would require, on the ministry's own arithmetic, more than ninety years.5

Owners cite three reasons, in roughly stable proportions across two decades of municipal surveys: the cost of a full retrofit (median ¥6.8M for a small mixed-use block); the disruption to tenants (an average of 14 weeks of partial vacancy); and the suspicion, frequently expressed, that a diagnostic inspection will simply reduce a property's market value without producing the funds to fix it.6

Meridian's geocoded dataset — built from 18,247 individual inspection records released across four prefectures — shows the missing retrofits concentrated unevenly. Tokyo's 23 special wards account for 71,400 of the identified buildings. Osaka City contributes 38,900. Kobe, despite the 1995 disaster, still carries 22,600 pre-1981 structures with no completed retrofit on file.

The distribution within cities is sharper still. In Tokyo's Adachi, Katsushika and Arakawa wards — three of the lowest-income wards by median household — pre-1981 buildings are 2.3 to 3.1 times more likely to remain un-retrofitted than in central Chiyoda or Minato. The pattern repeats across our four-city sample with a Spearman correlation of −0.78 between ward median income and un-retrofitted building density.7
"You can map the unfixed buildings onto the rental market. They are where the renters are. The owners are elsewhere, and they are waiting."

The Ministry's own 2022 white paper, in a footnote on page 184, acknowledges this skew but attributes it to "the natural distribution of older building stock." Our dataset shows the relationship persists after controlling for building age, lot size and zoning category.8

Between 2005 and 2023, central and local governments together budgeted ¥2.14 trillion for seismic retrofit subsidies. Of that, ¥1.31 trillion — roughly 61 percent — was disbursed. The rest expired unused at the close of each fiscal cycle and reverted to general funds.9

The application process is the most consistent obstacle named by owners in our survey of 412 building managers. The standard subsidy file requires twelve forms, three professional certifications, two rounds of public notice, and — in 31 of the 47 prefectures — a co-signing tenant association resolution. Average elapsed time from application to first disbursement, across the four cities we examined, is 14 months.
"The paperwork is not a bug. It is the law's quiet way of rationing itself."

What follows, in the next three sections, is a building-by-building account of how the gap between policy and practice gets reproduced. We begin with the 1974 block in Higashinada — the one with the hairline crack — and trace its inspection record from 1996 to the meeting last March at which its owners' association, after the seventh attempt, voted again to defer.10
A three-year investigation combining records analysis, on-site survey work, and structured interviews across four metropolitan regions of Japan.

All quantitative claims in this feature are sourced to a primary record listed in the data citations. Anonymous quotations were granted only where named attribution would have created professional risk; the editor-in-chief reviewed each anonymity grant.
From the passage of the New Seismic Standard in 1981 to the Ministry's latest revised horizon of 2035, the compliance target has been re-defined seven times.
Reporting by Kenji Arima, Dario Vellutini and Sun-Hee Park. Data analysis by the Meridian Data Desk (L. Otani, R. Hadid). Edited by E. Brennan. Translation review: M. Tanaka, S. Park.

1Kobe City Building Safety Division, inspection record HG-1996-0431; field re-survey by Meridian, 22 May 2024.
2Aggregated from MLIT annual seismic compliance reports 2005–2023 and prefectural disclosures under Article 76 requests filed 2022–2024.
3MLIT internal projection memo, March 2024, "Revised Compliance Horizon for Pre-1981 Stock."
4Subsidy ranges vary by municipality; rates cited reflect FY2023 standard schedules in Tokyo, Osaka, Kobe and Sendai.
5MLIT internal memos dated 12 Sept 1998 and 04 Nov 2002, obtained via disclosure request 2023-114.
6Meridian owner survey, n = 412, conducted Feb–Jun 2024; cost figures inflation-adjusted to 2024 yen.
7Spearman ρ = −0.78, p < 0.001; ward-level analysis, four-city sample.
8MLIT White Paper on Building Safety 2022, p.184, n.6; Meridian regression controls described in methodology sidebar.
9Fiscal Affairs Bureau, "Lapsed Allocation Summary," FY2005–FY2023.
10Higashinada owners' association minutes, March 2024, obtained with consent of two participants.
1981New Seismic Standard (shin-taishin) enacted; lateral-load formulas rewritten.
1995Great Hanshin earthquake kills 6,434; 250,000 buildings damaged in Kobe.
1996Promotion of Earthquake-Resistant Retrofit Law passed; subsidy framework established.
2006First national compliance target set: 90 percent retrofit coverage by 2015.
2013Mandatory diagnostic for designated emergency-route buildings; target slips to 2020.
2018Osaka northern earthquake exposes 11,000 un-retrofitted school walls.
2022MLIT white paper concedes 14% gap; target re-set to 2025.
2024Internal memo extends compliance horizon to 2035 — 54 years after enactment.

Nagai, Urban Engineering, Tokyo Metropolitan University The Ministry's own 2022 white paper, in a footnote on page 184, acknowledges this skew but attributes it to "the natural distribution of older building stock." We begin with the 1974 block in Higashinada — the one with the hairline crack — and trace its inspection record from 1996 to the meeting last March at which its owners' association, after the seventh attempt, voted again to defer. 10 1 Kobe City Building Safety Division, inspection record HG-1996-0431; field re-survey by Meridian, 22 May 2024. 2 Aggregated from MLIT annual seismic compliance reports 2005–2023 and prefectural disclosures under Article 76 requests filed 2022–2024. 3 MLIT internal projection memo, March 2024, "Revised Compliance Horizon for Pre-1981 Stock." 4 Subsidy ranges vary by municipality; rates cited reflect FY2023 standard schedules in Tokyo, Osaka, Kobe and Sendai. 5 MLIT internal memos dated 12 Sept 1998 and 04 Nov 2002, obtained via disclosure request 2023-114. 6 Meridian owner survey, n = 412, conducted Feb–Jun 2024; cost figures inflation-adjusted to 2024 yen. 7 Spearman ρ = −0.78, p < 0.001; ward-level analysis, four-city sample. 8 MLIT White Paper on Building Safety 2022, p.184, n.6; Meridian regression controls described in methodology sidebar. 9 Fiscal Affairs Bureau, "Lapsed Allocation Summary," FY2005–FY2023. 10 Higashinada owners' association minutes, March 2024, obtained with consent of two participants. Methodology A three-year investigation combining records analysis, on-site survey work, and structured interviews across four metropolitan regions of Japan. Data Sources Prefectural Inspection Records 18,247 individual inspection files released by Tokyo, Osaka, Hyōgo and Miyagi prefectures, FY2005–FY2023. MLIT Disclosure Set Article 76 disclosure requests 2022-114, 2023-114, 2024-038 (subsidy allocation, lapsed funds, projection memos). Owner Survey n = 412 building managers, four-city sample, fielded Feb–Jun 2024; response rate 38.1%. Field Inspections 147 buildings visited by Meridian correspondents; visual condition coded against the JBDPA Level-1 diagnostic checklist. Interview Corpus 62 recorded interviews with ministry officials, prefectural officers, structural engineers, owners, and tenants. Geocoding & Cross-Reference Building footprints joined to ward-level census, zoning, and median-income tables (2020 Census, 2023 NTSEL). Anonymous quotations were granted only where named attribution would have created professional risk; the editor-in-chief reviewed each anonymity grant. Timeline Forty-three years of moving deadlines From the passage of the New Seismic Standard in 1981 to the Ministry's latest revised horizon of 2035, the compliance target has been re-defined seven times. 1981 New Seismic Standard ( shin-taishin ) enacted; lateral-load formulas rewritten. 1995 Great Hanshin earthquake kills 6,434; 250,000 buildings damaged in Kobe. 1996 Promotion of Earthquake-Resistant Retrofit Law passed; subsidy framework established. 2006 First national compliance target set: 90 percent retrofit coverage by 2015. 2013 Mandatory diagnostic for designated emergency-route buildings; target slips to 2020. 2018 Osaka northern earthquake exposes 11,000 un-retrofitted school walls. 2022 MLIT white paper concedes 14% gap; target re-set to 2025. 2024 Internal memo extends compliance horizon to 2035 — 54 years after enactment. Figures Four views of the retrofit gap 3F 2F 1F CRACK PRE-1981 REINF. CONC. Figure 1 · Cross-section FIG. 1 — Typical pre-1981 three-storey reinforced-concrete apartment block with the lateral-shear failure pattern observed in 41 of 147 surveyed structures. The vertical crack propagates along the load-bearing wall at the south elevation; the foundation, poured to pre-1981 specifications, lacks tie reinforcement at column bases. ADACHI LOW INCOME ← → HIGH HIGH DENSITY Figure 2 · Ward map FIG. 2 — Density of un-retrofitted pre-1981 buildings across Tokyo's 23 special wards, plotted against ward-level median household income. The deepest-red cells — Adachi, Katsushika, Arakawa — carry between 2.3 and 3.1 times the un-retrofitted density of central Chiyoda and Minato. 90 60 30 0 TOKYO OSAKA KOBE SENDAI FUKU. % PRE-1981 STOCK Figure 3 · Target vs. actual FIG. 3 — Government retrofit target (slate) versus measured compliance (red) across five metropolitan regions, FY2023. The arithmetic mean compliance rate of 36.4% falls below the 90% national target by a margin that has not narrowed since 2018. BUILDING AGE (YEARS) RETROFIT GAP HIGH RISK Figure 4 · Risk matrix FIG. 4 — Building age plotted against compliance gap; bubble area represents tenant population at risk. Structures clustering in the upper-right quadrant — older than 50 years and with the widest gap between diagnostic findings and completed retrofits — house an estimated 1.2 million residents. Reporting by Kenji Arima, Dario Vellutini and Sun-Hee Park. Data analysis by the Meridian Data Desk (L. Otani, R. Hadid). Edited by E. Brennan. Translation review: M. Tanaka, S. Park.
